sections.paragraph:
δήπου adv. (Hom. et al.; PCairZen 454, 4; Jos., C. Ap. 1, 127; Tat. 5, 2; Ath., R. 70, 23 al.) like μέν, γέ, δή a marker of emphasis, but the suffix που is rhetorically significant, for it softens the process of inference, while yet strongly asserting the conclusion, by graciously taking the auditor into the logical process, somewhat in the sense ‘one would agree, I’m sure’ of course, surely Hb 2:16 (so also Hom., Trag. et al., s. Denniston 267–68; B-D-F §441, 3).

adv. adv. = adverb, adverbially

Hom Hom , VIII b.c.—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

PCairZen PCairZen = Zenon Papyri, Catalogue général . . . du Musée du Caire—List 4

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

Tat Tat , II a.d.—List 5

Ath. Ath. = Athenagoras, II a.d.—List 5

  1. al. =alibi (elsewhere), aliter (otherwise), alii (others)

Trag.

Trag. = writer (s) of tragedy

trag. = tragedy, writer (s) of tragedy

Denniston Denniston = JD., The Greek Particles—List 6

B-D-F B-D-F = FBlass, ADebrunner, RFunk, A Greek Grammar of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 223
